questions.question: What force keeps us on the ground?
questions.explanation: Gravity (gravitational force) is the force of attraction between the Earth and all objects on it. It keeps us grounded.

questions.question: Newton's Second Law of Motion states that Force = :
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma (Force = Mass × Acceleration). Greater force = greater acceleration for the same mass.

questions.question: The 'Law of Gravitation' was proposed by:
questions.explanation: Sir Isaac Newton formulated the Universal Law of Gravitation (1687): F = Gm1m2/r², stating that every object attracts every other object with a force proportional to their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

questions.question: The SI unit of pressure is:
questions.explanation: The SI unit of pressure is Pascal (Pa), named after Blaise Pascal. 1 Pa = 1 Newton per square metre (N/m²).

questions.question: Newton's First Law of Motion states that:
options: A body at rest or uniform motion continues unless acted upon by an external force
Every action has an equal and opposite reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia): A body continues in its state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line unless acted upon by an external unbalanced force. This is why we feel a jerk when a bus suddenly brakes.

questions.question: Newton's Second Law of Motion (F = ma) means:
options: Force equals mass multiplied by acceleration
Force is inversely proportional to mass
All bodies accelerate at the same rate
questions.explanation: F = ma — the net force acting on a body equals the product of its mass and acceleration. A larger force produces greater acceleration; a larger mass requires more force to achieve the same acceleration.

questions.question: Newton's Third Law of Motion states:
options: For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Third Law: For every action (force), there is an equal and opposite reaction. Example: rocket engines push exhaust gas backward (action), gas pushes rocket forward (reaction).

questions.question: The SI unit of force is:
questions.explanation: The SI unit of force is Newton (N). 1 N = 1 kg·m/s². 1 Newton is the force that gives a 1 kg mass an acceleration of 1 m/s². Named after Sir Isaac Newton.

questions.question: The acceleration due to gravity on Earth is approximately:
questions.explanation: The acceleration due to gravity (g) on Earth's surface is approximately 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s²). It varies slightly with latitude (less at equator, more at poles) and altitude (decreases with height).

questions.question: Which law states that the total momentum of a closed system remains constant?
questions.explanation: Law of Conservation of Momentum: The total linear momentum of a closed system (no external forces) remains constant. Total momentum before collision = Total momentum after collision.

questions.question: Archimedes' Principle states that:
options: A body immersed in fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of displaced fluid
Fluids flow from high to low pressure
questions.explanation: Archimedes' Principle: When a body is fully or partially submerged in a fluid, it experiences a buoyant force equal to the weight of fluid displaced. This explains why ships float and hot air balloons rise.

questions.question: Bernoulli's Principle states that:
options: In a fluid, increased velocity causes decreased pressure
Buoyancy equals weight of displaced fluid
questions.explanation: Bernoulli's Principle: In a streamlined fluid flow, as velocity increases, pressure decreases. This explains lift in airplane wings, the Venturi effect, and why shower curtains are pulled inward.

questions.question: Work done is measured in:
questions.explanation: Work = Force × Distance. The SI unit of work is Joule (J). 1 J = 1 N × 1 m. Work is done only when a force displaces an object in the direction of the force.

questions.question: Black holes are formed when:
options: Massive stars collapse after exhausting their nuclear fuel (core collapse → extreme gravity from which light cannot escape)
questions.explanation: Black holes form when massive stars (>20 solar masses) exhaust nuclear fuel and their cores collapse under gravity. The gravity is so strong that even light cannot escape (escape velocity > c). Detected through gravitational effects.

questions.question: The pressure at which blood is pumped out of the heart is called:
options: Blood pressure — systolic (contraction) over diastolic (relaxation), normal 120/80 mmHg
questions.explanation: Blood pressure is the force exerted by blood on artery walls. Systolic pressure (top number) = when heart contracts (~120 mmHg). Diastolic pressure (bottom number) = when heart relaxes (~80 mmHg). High BP (hypertension) > 140/90 mmHg.

questions.question: A lactometer is used to measure:
options: The purity/density of milk (to detect adulteration with water)
questions.explanation: A lactometer measures the density (specific gravity) of milk. Pure milk has specific gravity ~1.028-1.033. When water is added, density decreases. It is based on Archimedes' principle — the lactometer floats at different levels depending on milk density.

questions.question: A body in uniform circular motion has:
options: Constant speed but changing velocity (centripetal acceleration directed toward center)
questions.explanation: In uniform circular motion, speed (magnitude of velocity) is constant but velocity direction continuously changes. This requires centripetal acceleration = v²/r directed toward the center. The centripetal force provides this acceleration (tension, gravity, friction, etc.).

questions.question: The escape velocity from Earth's surface is approximately:
options: 11.2 km/s (about 40,000 km/h) — minimum speed to escape Earth's gravitational field
questions.explanation: Escape velocity from Earth = √(2gR) ≈ 11.2 km/s. This is the minimum speed needed for an object to escape Earth's gravity without further propulsion. Orbital velocity (first cosmic velocity) ≈ 7.9 km/s for low Earth orbit.

questions.question: Newton's Law of Gravitation states that gravitational force is:
options: Directly proportional to product of masses and inversely proportional to square of distance (F = Gm₁m₂/r²)
questions.explanation: Newton's Universal Law of Gravitation: F = Gm₁m₂/r², where G = 6.674×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg² is the gravitational constant. The force is attractive, acts between all masses, and decreases with square of distance.

questions.question: When a ball is thrown upward, at the highest point:
options: Velocity = 0 but acceleration = g (9.8 m/s²) downward (still under gravity)
questions.explanation: At the highest point of projectile motion, the vertical velocity component = 0 (ball momentarily stops going up). However, gravitational acceleration (g = 9.8 m/s² downward) acts at all times — it is g that decelerates the ball going up and accelerates it coming down.

questions.question: A pulley is used to:
options: Change the direction of force and/or reduce the effort needed to lift a load (mechanical advantage)
questions.explanation: A single fixed pulley changes the direction of force (effort direction changes, but magnitude stays same — no mechanical advantage). A movable pulley halves the effort needed (MA=2). Combination block-and-tackle systems provide higher mechanical advantages for cranes and elevators.

questions.question: The theory of relativity (Special and General) was developed by:
options: Albert Einstein — Special Relativity (1905, E=mc²) and General Relativity (1915, gravity as curvature of spacetime)
questions.explanation: Einstein: Special Relativity (1905) — at high speeds, time dilates, length contracts, mass increases; E=mc² (mass-energy equivalence). General Relativity (1915) — gravity is curvature of spacetime by mass/energy. Predicted: gravitational waves, black holes, gravitational lensing.

questions.question: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is primarily based on:
options: Machine learning — algorithms that improve with data; deep learning uses neural networks mimicking the brain
questions.explanation: Modern AI: Machine Learning (ML) — algorithms learn patterns from data. Deep Learning (DL): multi-layer artificial neural networks (ANNs) — basis for image recognition, language models (GPT), voice assistants. Natural Language Processing (NLP), Computer Vision, and Reinforcement Learning are sub-fields.

questions.question: The phenomenon of 'capillary action' is due to:
options: Adhesion (water attracted to glass/plant cell walls) and cohesion (water-water attraction via H bonds) — water rises in narrow tubes against gravity
questions.explanation: Capillary action: water rises in narrow tubes (capillaries) against gravity. Due to: adhesion (water attracted to tube walls) > gravity on that water column + cohesion (water molecules attract each other). Occurs in plant xylem vessels (helps water ascent), paper (chromatography), and wicks (candles, lamps).

questions.question: Phototropism in plants means:
options: Growth of plant parts in response to light — shoots grow toward light (positive phototropism); roots grow away from light (negative phototropism)
questions.explanation: Tropism: directed growth response to a stimulus. Phototropism: light. Geotropism (gravitropism): gravity (roots down, shoots up). Hydrotropism: water (roots toward water). Thigmotropism: touch (tendrils wrap around supports). Chemotropism: chemicals (pollen tube grows toward ovule). Controlled by auxin redistribution.

questions.question: Peristalsis is the movement that:
options: Propels food through the digestive tract — wave-like contraction and relaxation of smooth muscles in esophagus, stomach, and intestines
questions.explanation: Peristalsis: wave-like contractions of circular and longitudinal smooth muscles in the digestive tract. Circular muscles contract behind a food bolus; longitudinal muscles relax → bolus moves forward. Occurs in esophagus (pushes food to stomach even against gravity), stomach (churning), small/large intestines. Also in ureters (urine to bladder).

questions.question: The phenomenon of 'surface tension' in water explains:
options: Why water strider insects can walk on water, why water forms droplets, and why a needle can float on still water surface
Why water has high boiling point
questions.explanation: Surface tension: cohesive forces between water molecules at the surface create a 'skin' that resists deformation. Surface molecules have fewer neighbors → net inward pull. Water has high surface tension (72.8 mN/m at 25°C) due to H-bonding. Soap lowers surface tension → bubbles, better wetting.

questions.question: The study of the human mind and behavior is:
options: Psychology (psyche = mind/soul; logos = study) — scientific study of mental processes and behavior
questions.explanation: Psychology: scientific study of behavior and mental processes (perception, cognition, emotion, personality, behavior). Wilhelm Wundt (1879) opened first experimental psychology lab (Leipzig). Major approaches: Freudian/psychoanalytic, behavioral, cognitive, humanistic, and biological. Psychiatry: medical specialty treating mental disorders (uses medications).

questions.question: Pascal's Law states that:
options: Pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted equally in all directions (basis of hydraulic systems)
Pressure increases with velocity in flowing fluids
Buoyant force equals weight of displaced fluid
Gases expand to fill their container
questions.explanation: Pascal's Law: pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted equally and without change throughout the fluid (in all directions). Applications: hydraulic press, hydraulic brakes, hydraulic jacks, dentist's chairs. A small force can lift huge weights.

questions.question: The principle behind an aircraft wing generating lift is:
options: Bernoulli's Principle — air moves faster over curved upper surface, creating lower pressure on top than below, generating lift
questions.explanation: Aircraft wings (airfoils) use Bernoulli's Principle: air over the curved upper surface travels faster than air below, creating lower pressure above the wing. The pressure difference generates an upward lift force. Newton's 3rd Law also contributes (wing deflects air downward).

questions.question: Isaac Newton's contribution to mathematics includes:
options: Development of calculus (simultaneously and independently with Leibniz) — also discovered binomial theorem and made contributions to infinite series
questions.explanation: Isaac Newton (1642-1727): Principia Mathematica (1687) — laws of motion and gravitation. Mathematics: co-invented calculus (he called it 'method of fluxions'; Leibniz's notation dx/dy is used today), generalized binomial theorem, developed infinite series. Physics: optics (white light decomposition), reflecting telescope.

questions.question: A car accelerates uniformly from rest to a speed of 20 m/s in 10 seconds. What is its acceleration?
questions.explanation: Acceleration = (final velocity - initial velocity) / time = (20 - 0) / 10 = 2 m/s².

questions.question: Which of the following is true for a body moving with uniform circular motion?
questions.explanation: In uniform circular motion, speed remains constant but velocity changes due to direction change, resulting in centripetal acceleration and force.

questions.question: If a ball is thrown vertically upward with a velocity of 30 m/s, what is the maximum height reached? (Take g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Using v² = u² - 2gh, at maximum height v = 0, so h = u²/(2g) = 900 / 20 = 45 m.

questions.question: The slope of a velocity-time graph gives:
questions.explanation: Slope of velocity-time graph is change in velocity per unit time, which is acceleration.

questions.question: Newton's first law of motion is also known as:
questions.explanation: Newton's first law states that an object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by an external force, which is the principle of inertia.

questions.question: A train moving at 72 km/h applies brakes and comes to a stop in 10 seconds. The retardation is:
questions.explanation: 72 km/h = 20 m/s, retardation = (0 - 20) / 10 = -2 m/s², magnitude is 2 m/s².

questions.question: For a freely falling body, the distance covered in the nth second is proportional to:
questions.explanation: Distance in nth second = u + (g/2)(2n - 1). For free fall u = 0, so it is proportional to (2n - 1).

questions.question: Which of the following pairs represents the correct SI units of velocity and acceleration?
questions.explanation: SI unit of velocity is m/s and of acceleration is m/s².

questions.question: A body of mass 2 kg moving with a velocity of 10 m/s is brought to rest in 2 seconds by a constant force. The magnitude of the force is:
questions.explanation: Acceleration = (0 - 10) / 2 = -5 m/s², so force = mass × acceleration = 2 × 5 = 10 N.

questions.question: The unit of momentum is:
questions.explanation: Momentum = mass × velocity (kg m/s) is equivalent to Newton-second (N s) because force = dp/dt.

questions.question: A car accelerates uniformly from rest to a velocity of 20 m/s in 10 seconds. What is the distance covered by the car during this time?
questions.explanation: Using s = ut + ½at², with u=0, a=2 m/s² (from v=u+at), t=10 s gives s = 0 + ½×2×100 = 100 m.

questions.question: Which of the following physical quantities is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity has both magnitude and direction, making it a vector, while speed, distance, and mass are scalars.

questions.question: An object is thrown vertically upward with an initial velocity of 30 m/s. What is the maximum height reached? (Take g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Using v² = u² - 2gh, at maximum height v=0, so h = u²/(2g) = 900/20 = 45 m.

questions.question: Newton's first law of motion is also known as the law of
questions.explanation: Newton's first law states that an object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by an external force, hence called the law of inertia.

questions.question: A force of 20 N acts on a body of mass 5 kg for 4 seconds. The change in momentum of the body is
questions.explanation: Change in momentum = Force × time = 20 N × 4 s = 80 kg m/s.

questions.question: A ball is dropped from a height of 80 m. How long does it take to reach the ground? (Take g = 10 m/s²)
When a moving bus suddenly stops, passengers are thrown forward due to
questions.explanation: Passengers continue moving forward due to inertia of motion when the bus stops suddenly, as per Newton's first law.

questions.question: A ball is thrown vertically upward with an initial velocity of 20 m/s. Neglecting air resistance, what is the maximum height reached by the ball? (Take g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Using v² = u² - 2gh, at maximum height v=0, so h = u²/(2g) = (20²)/(2*10) = 20 m.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about a body under uniform acceleration from rest: 1. The velocity is directly proportional to time. 2. The distance covered is directly proportional to the square of time. Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: For uniform acceleration from rest, v = at (proportional to time) and s = 1/2 at² (proportional to t²); both statements are correct.

questions.question: A particle starts from rest and moves with constant acceleration. It covers a distance of 45 m in the 5th second of its motion. What is the acceleration?
questions.explanation: Distance in nth second = u + a/2(2n-1). With u=0, n=5 gives 45 = a/2 * 9 => a=10 m/s².

questions.question: Two trains of lengths 200 m and 300 m are moving on parallel tracks at speeds 72 km/h and 54 km/h respectively in opposite directions. The time taken to completely cross each other is:
questions.explanation: Relative speed = (72+54) km/h = 35 m/s; total distance = 500 m; time = 500/35 = 14.3 s.

questions.question: The velocity-time graph of a particle moving along a straight line is a straight line inclined to the time axis. The particle has:
questions.explanation: A straight line on v-t graph indicates constant slope, hence constant acceleration.

questions.question: A body is moving in a circular path of radius R with constant speed v. The magnitude of centripetal acceleration is:
questions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration is given by a_c = v²/R directed towards the center.

questions.question: A projectile is fired at an angle θ with the horizontal. If its range is R and maximum height is H, then which of the following is correct for complementary angles (θ and 90°-θ)?
options: R is same, H is same
R is same, H is different
R is different, H is same
R is different, H is different
questions.explanation: Range for complementary angles is same; maximum height differs because H ∝ sin²θ, which is not equal for θ and 90°-θ except at 45°.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding Newton's first law of motion: 1. It defines inertia. 2. It gives a quantitative measure of force. Which is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Newton's first law defines inertia (qualitative), but force is quantified by second law; only statement 1 is correct.

questions.question: A block of mass 2 kg is placed on a rough inclined plane of inclination 30°. The coefficient of static friction is 0.4. The minimum force required to prevent the block from sliding down is (take g = 10 m/s² and sin30° = 0.5, cos30° ≈ 0.866):
questions.explanation: Downward component = mg sinθ = 10 N; static friction maximum = μmg cosθ = 6.928 N; net force needed = 10 - 6.928 ≈ 3.07 N upward.

questions.question: A ball is thrown vertically upward with a speed of 20 m/s. How long does it take to return to the starting point? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Time of flight = 2u/g = 2×20/10 = 4 s.

questions.question: The displacement-time graph of a moving particle is a parabola opening downwards. The motion of the particle is:
questions.explanation: A parabolic s-t graph with downward opening indicates decreasing slope over time, hence uniform retardation (negative constant acceleration).

questions.question:
Assertion (A): In a projectile motion, the horizontal component of velocity remains constant.
Reason (R): There is no acceleration in the horizontal direction.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: In projectile motion, horizontal acceleration is zero, so horizontal velocity remains constant; R correctly explains A.

questions.question: A car accelerates uniformly from rest to 20 m/s in 10 s, travels at constant speed for 10 s, and then decelerates uniformly to rest in 5 s. What is the average speed of the car over the entire journey?
questions.explanation: Distances: acceleration 100 m, constant 200 m, deceleration 50 m; total 350 m in 25 s gives 14 m/s.

questions.question: Two bodies of masses m and 2m are dropped from heights h and 2h respectively. Neglecting air resistance, the ratio of times taken to reach the ground is:
questions.explanation: Time of free fall t ∝ √h, so ratio t1:t2 = √h : √(2h) = 1:√2.

questions.question: A particle moves in a circular path of radius r with constant speed v. Which of the following is correct about its acceleration?
options: It is constant in magnitude but direction changes
It is constant in both magnitude and direction
questions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration has constant magnitude v²/r but always points towards the centre, so its direction continuously changes.

questions.question: A ball is projected vertically upward with speed 20 m/s. The time taken to return to the point of projection is (g = 10 m/s²):
questions.explanation: Time to maximum height = u/g = 2 s, so total time of flight = 2 × 2 = 4 s.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The slope of velocity-time graph gives acceleration.
Reason (R): Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Slope of v–t graph is dv/dt = acceleration; R correctly defines acceleration and explains A.

questions.question: A train of length 100 m moving at 20 m/s crosses a bridge of length 200 m. The time taken to completely cross the bridge is:
questions.explanation: Total distance to cross = train length + bridge length = 300 m; time = 300/20 = 15 s.

questions.question: Under what condition does the average velocity equal the instantaneous velocity?
questions.explanation: Average velocity equals instantaneous velocity when velocity is constant, which implies zero acceleration; both conditions hold.

questions.question: A stone is thrown horizontally from a height of 45 m with a speed of 10 m/s. The time it takes to hit the ground is (g = 10 m/s²):
questions.explanation: Vertical motion: h = ½gt² → t = √(2h/g) = √(90/10) = 3 s.

questions.question: In uniform circular motion, which of the following remains constant?
questions.explanation: Speed is constant, so kinetic energy (½mv²) is constant; velocity and acceleration direction change, displacement varies.

questions.question: A particle moves along the x-axis with velocity v = 3t^2 - 12t + 9 (m/s). At t=0, it is at the origin. The total distance traveled by the particle from the first instant when it comes to rest to the second instant when it comes to rest is:
questions.explanation: Velocity zero at t=1s and t=3s; position function x = t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t gives x(1)=4, x(3)=0; distance between is |0-4| = 4 m.
